title: Solanum ruderale
taxonomy: Solanum ruderale is a species within the family Solanaceae, commonly known as the nightshade family. This family includes a wide range of plant species, many of which are economically important, such as tomatoes and potatoes. Solanum ruderale is further classified within the genus Solanum, which comprises over 1,500 species. The plant is native to South America, where it is found growing in a variety of environments. As a member of the nightshade family, Solanum ruderale is a dicotyledonous plant, meaning it has two seed leaves when it germinates.

Solanum ruderale is a plant that should be handled with caution, as its leaves and seeds contain solanine, a toxic compound similar to nicotine. intro: Solanum ruderale, also known as tree tobacco or wild tobacco, is a perennial plant native to South America, belonging to the Solanaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in the treatment of various ailments such as wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Solanum ruderale include solanine, solamargine, and solanidine, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is commonly used to prepare teas, infusions, and topical creams, with the dried leaves being the most widely used part for medicinal purposes.
